coredata.dc:title: Short wavelength light absorption and conversion films containing carbon nanodots for light damage prevention
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Bin
Ni, Jiaxin
Wang, Yuning
Sun, Lianhang
Bai, Yibing
Han, Shiyan
Chen, Zhijun
Zhang, Yahui
Gao, Fugang
Yu, Wenji
Li, Shujun
coredata.dc:description: The carbon nanodots (CNDs) can absorb short wavelength light and enable excited electrons to achieve light conversion to long wavelength emission by radiative transition, while accompanying the non-radiative transition of excited electrons, in order to block the short wavelength light. The CNDs were prepared from chitosan as the carbon source, and then mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare CNDs-based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs) containing different proportions of CNDs. Images were clearly visible through CND-OBFs under both close and long-distance observation, demonstrating good transparency. The CND-OBFs were shown to block both UV light from sunlight and harmful blue light from computers, mobile phones and white light-emitting diodes. The CND-OBFs can thus be used to effectively block short wavelength light. Samples of wood were covered with different CND-OBFs and the wood surfaces were then irradiated with UV light (340 nm, 80 W). The results show that different CND-OBFs block short wavelength light to different extents; CND-OBF containing 15.7 % CNDs can completely block short wavelength light through absorption and conversion, protecting the wood samples from discoloration. This work provides a new research approach in which CNDs are used to protect wood by blocking short wavelength light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carbon nanodots
Optical blocking film
wood protection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CNDs based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program ( 2021YFD2200601 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32171715 ) and the Hei Long Jiang Postdoctoral Foundation ( LBH-Q21003 ). The authors are grateful for the funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and high resolution TEM (inset) images of CNDs; (b) Particle size histogram of CNDs; (c) XRD spectrum of CNDs; (d) Raman spectrum of CNDs; (e) Full XPS spectrum of CNDs; (f) C1s XPS spectrum of CNDs; (g) O1s XPS spectrum of CNDs; (h) N1s XPS spectrum of CNDs; (i) FTIR spectrum of CNDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of solutions of CNDs (0.01–0.8 mg/mL); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of solutions of CNDs (0.01–0.2 mg/mL) (Ex = 360 nm); (c) Normalized photoluminescence spectra of solutions of CNDs (0.01–4 mg/mL); (d) Intensity of emission of solutions of CNDs excited at 360 nm for 3000 s; (e) Reversible temperature-dependence of fluorescence intensity of solution of CNDs between 25 °C and 70 °C (Ex = 360 nm); (f) Fluorescence decay curves of solutions of CNDs (0.01 mg/mL, Ex = 330 nm).

Images printed on paper using fluorescent ink made from solution of CNDs, illuminated with (a) natural sunlight and (b) UV light (365 nm); (c) Changes in fluorescence intensity of solution of CNDs (20 μg/mL) in the presence of different cations (100 μM); (d) Linear relationship of I0/I vs. concentration of Fe3+ ions (1–100 μM) (I0 and I are fluorescence intensities without and with Fe3+ ions, respectively).

(a) Photographs of CND-OBFs (0–31.8 % CNDs) under sunlight (top) and UV light (365 nm) (bottom); (b) Photographs of the letters ‘‘Chitosan” (top) and pictures of a real object (bottom) taken through CND-OBFs (0–31.8 % CNDs); (c) Transmittance of CND-OBFs (0–31.8 % CNDs); (d) UV (395 nm) light blocking ability of CND-OBFs containing different amounts of CNDs; (e) Blue (450 nm) light blocking ability of CND-OBFs containing different amounts of CNDs; (f–h) CIE chromaticity coordinates of CND-OBFs containing different proportions of CNDs blocking blue light emitted by computer screen, phone screen and modulator tube.

(a–c) Pictures of wood before (above), during (middle) and after (bottom) aging. (a) Balsa wood, (b) white maple and (c) basswood; Response of wood (d) △L* and (e) △E* to different CND-OBFs under UV light (365 nm); (f) Diagram showing mechanism of UV blocking.
Radar map comparison of CND-OBF (15.7 % CNDs) and other UV protection films (values close to the outer ring indicate desirable properties).
Schematic showing (a) Formation of CNDs and CND-based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs); (b) Use of CND-OBF as a protective coating for wood.

head.ce:title: Short wavelength light absorption and conversion films containing carbon nanodots for light damage prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Transline Technology Co., Ltd., Changzhou 213100, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon nanodots (CNDs) can absorb short wavelength light and enable excited electrons to achieve light conversion to long wavelength emission by radiative transition, while accompanying the non-radiative transition of excited electrons, in order to block the short wavelength light. The CNDs were prepared from chitosan as the carbon source, and then mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare CNDs-based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs) containing different proportions of CNDs. Images were clearly visible through CND-OBFs under both close and long-distance observation, demonstrating good transparency. The CND-OBFs were shown to block both UV light from sunlight and harmful blue light from computers, mobile phones and white light-emitting diodes. The CND-OBFs can thus be used to effectively block short wavelength light. Samples of wood were covered with different CND-OBFs and the wood surfaces were then irradiated with UV light (340 nm, 80 W). The results show that different CND-OBFs block short wavelength light to different extents; CND-OBF containing 15.7 % CNDs can completely block short wavelength light through absorption and conversion, protecting the wood samples from discoloration. This work provides a new research approach in which CNDs are used to protect wood by blocking short wavelength light.

The carbon nanodots (CNDs) based optical blocking film (CND-OBF) containing concentration-dependent long wavelength emitting CNDs was developed and used to protect wood from damage caused by short wavelength light.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of their environmental friendliness and excellent optical properties, carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been widely used in sensing [1,2], white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) [3–6], bioimaging [7,8], anti-counterfeiting [9–11], catalysis [12–15] and other fields [6,16–19]. Absorption and conversion of light are acknowledged to be the main contributors to photoluminescence by CNDs. CNDs show excitation- and concentration-dependent emission and, when excited by short wavelength light, they can absorb photons, leading to excitation of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. Subsequently, the excited electrons transition in two ways, one is non-radiative transition release, and the other is radiative transition returning to the ground state, resulting in long wavelength emission, i.e., short wavelength light is absorbed and converted into long wavelength light emission [20–22]. This interesting property of CNDs has aroused considerable interest in their use as blockers of short wavelength light [20,23–26]. Wood is an important and indispensable building material and is also important for achieving the goal of carbon neutrality [27,28]. After exposure to sunlight for long periods of time, the lignin in cut wood is degraded by short wavelength light, resulting in color changes and reduced mechanical strength [29,30]. Because of this, increasing the resistance of wood to ultraviolet (UV) aging has long been an important topic in wood protection. Ideally, a method to directly absorb and block UV rays and protect the wood from damage caused by short wavelength light, without changing any other properties, would be used before the wood is processed into finished products. In this article, we propose using the light absorption and conversion properties of CNDs to protect wood from the damage caused by UV light.

Natural resources have been widely studied because, among other advantages, they are abundant, renewable and non-toxic [31–35]. Because of these advantages, out of the many carbon sources, natural resources and their derivative have gradually become one of the main sources of carbon in CNDs research [9,20,36–38]. Chitosan is an important polymeric polysaccharide that is obtained by deacetylation of the natural resource chitin [39]. It is widely used because of abundant reserves, non-toxicity and good biocompatibility, and has been gradually developed as the carbon source [22,40], Therefore, we chose chitosan as the carbon source to develop a method of producing CNDs using a one-step hydrothermal carbonization process. Taking advantage of their light absorption and conversion properties, we compounded the CNDs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to obtain CND-based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs). These films are able to absorb and block short wavelength light (Scheme 1 a) and can be used to protect wood from short wavelength light (Scheme 1b).

A mixture of chitosan (2 g) and deionized water (70 mL) was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. The reaction mixture was then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was collected, freeze-dried and ground to obtain the carbon nanodots (CNDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CNDs based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs)
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (MW = 1750, 1 g) was placed in a 100 mL beaker and deionized water (25 mL) was added. The beaker was sealed with plastic wrap, placed in a 95 °C constant temperature water bath, stirred for 1 h and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to provide an aqueous solution of PVA. An aqueous solution of CNDs was added to the solution of PVA and the mixed solution was magnetically stirred (400 r/min) for 1 h and then sonicated for 1 h. The mixture was then poured at room temperature into a plastic petri dish (diameter 10 cm) to produce the CND-OBF by the tape casting method, and dried at room temperature to form CND-based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs). Using this general method, CND-OBFs containing different proportions (0 %, 0.9 %, 4.5 %, 8.5 %, 15.7 %, 21.9 %, 27.2 % and 31.8 %) of CNDs were obtained.

Scanning transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed that the CNDs are spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 1 a), with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon (Fig. 1a inset) [9,20,41]. The particle size distribution of the CNDs was in the range of 2.33–8.84 nm (Fig. 1b), with an average particle size of 5.14 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis showed a strong diffraction peak at 20.9° and a weak diffraction peak at 41.1° (Fig. 1c), which were attributed to the (002) and (100) crystal planes [42,43], respectively. The Raman spectrum of the CNDs had peaks in the D band (1355 cm−1) and G band (1545 cm−1) (Fig. 1d), corresponding to sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons and indicating that the CNDs have a graphitized structure [44,45]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated that the CNDs were mainly composed of C (71.32 %), O (18.56 %) and N (10.12 %) (Fig. 1e). The high-resolution C1s spectrum showed peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV and 287.8 eV (Fig. 1f), corresponding to C-C/C=C [46], C-O/C-N [46,47] and C=O/C=N groups [46,48], respectively. Signals at 530.6 eV and 532.0 eV in the O1s spectrum were attributed to C=O [49,50] and C-O [42,49] groups (Fig. 1g). Two bands in the N1s spectrum at 399.3 eV and 401.1 eV were attributed to amino N [22,51] and graphite N [48] (Fig. 1h), respectively. The Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 1i) showed peaks at ∼3185 cm−1, 2867–2923 cm−1, 1662 cm−1, 1565 cm−1 and 1071 cm−1, which were attributed to the absorption peaks of -NH2/-OH [22], -CH3/-CH2 [47], C=O [22], C=C/C=N [22] and C-OH [20] groups, respectively. Both XPS and FTIR spectroscopy confirmed the presence of N both on the surface and inside the graphitized carbon core. These results indicate the presence of functional groups, such as amino, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, on the surface of CNDs, which not only enhance the water solubility of the CNDs, but also may lead to diversification of the surface states of the CNDs [20].

Having determined the structure of the CNDs, we next investigated their optical properties. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CNDs aqueous solution exhibited obvious absorption peaks at 197 nm and 280 nm, with absorption extending to 600 nm (Fig. 2 a). These absorption peaks were attributed to the π-π* electron transition of C=C bonds and n-π* electron transitions of C=O/C=N bonds [23,49]. As the concentration of the solution was increased, the absorption was noticeably enhanced and broadened (Fig. 2a and Fig. S1), which may caused the red shift of emission [21,52,53]. The fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solutions of CNDs were all excitation-dependent and not affected by concentration (Fig. S2). The emission intensity increased with increasing concentration, reaching a maximum when the concentration was 0.2 mg/mL (Fig. 2b and Fig. S3). As the concentration was increased further, however, the emission intensity decreased (Fig. S4), potentially due to the factors such as aggregation-caused quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, re-absorption, or internal filtration effect [9,20,41]. When the concentration of the solution was increased from 0.01 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL, the optimal excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength were also red-shifted from 330 nm to 470 nm (Fig. S5) and from 425 nm to 555 nm (Fig. 2c and Fig. S5), respectively. And the excitation spectrum was consistent with the emission spectrum (Fig. S6). The probable reason for the large red-shift in emission was that the overlap of short wavelength emission and absorption (Fig. S7) was weakened due to resonant energy transfer [41]. Additionally, excitation dependence could also lead to the significant shift in the emission wavelength of CNDs [54], as it further enhanced the long wavelength emission after the re-absorption process [41]. These results demonstrate that the emission of aqueous solutions of CNDs is concentration-dependent, which allows the CNDs to absorb short wavelength (≤450 nm) light, and convert this to longer wavelength light emission [20,55].

The stability of aqueous solutions of CNDs was examined next. After continuous excitation at 360 nm for 50 min, the fluorescence emission intensity decreased less than 5 % (Fig. 2d), demonstrating that solutions of CNDs have strong resistance to photobleaching. The fluorescence emission intensity of an aqueous solution of CNDs decreased with increasing temperature (Fig. S8), which is thought to be closely associated with enhanced nonradiative relaxation [55–57]. It is worth mentioning that a solution of CNDs showed reversible fluorescence emission over five cycles of heating (70 °C) and cooling (25 °C) (Fig. 2e), indicating that the solution of CNDs has good temperature cycle stability. The fluorescence lifetime decay curve of a solution of CNDs exhibited double exponential fitting (Fig. 2f and Table S1), indicating the presence of different fluorescent chromophores [9,21,55,58]. The fluorescence lifetime of a solution of CNDs was estimated to be 7.03 ns, with a quantum yield of 6.04 %.

Ink printing and metal ion detection are two of the most commonly demonstrated applications of CNDs. Here, a solution of CNDs was used to print patterns, which were invisible under natural light (Fig. 3 a) but were clearly revealed as blue fluorescent patterns when illuminated with a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3b). The effect of different ions (cations and anions) on the fluorescence intensity of an aqueous solution of CNDs was also investigated (Fig. 3c and Fig. S9a). Of the ions tested, Fe3+ had a marked quenching effect whereas other cations and anions (Figs. S9b and c) had only a weak effect or no effect. As the concentration of Fe3+ was increased, the fluorescence quenching was gradually enhanced (Fig. S10). This is because hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CNDs interact with Fe3+, resulting in the transfer of electrons from the CNDs to the d orbitals of Fe3+ [55,59], thereby reducing radiative transitions and inhibiting exciton recombination. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was 7.79 μM (Fig. 3d). These results show that solutions of CNDs can be used in fluorescent ink printing and for the detection of trace amounts of Fe3+.

Building on the ability of aqueous solutions of CNDs to absorb short wavelength light and convert it into long wavelength light emission, we next mixed CNDs with PVA (the similar function of water) and obtained CND-based optical blocking films (CND-OBFs) using the tape casting method. The cross-sectional SEM images of both pure PVA film and CND-OBFs revealed the thickness in the range of 76–83 μm (Fig. S11). As the proportion of CNDs in the film was increased, the color of the film under natural light gradually changed from colorless to brown and the color under 365 nm UV light changed from blue to dark yellow-green (Fig. 4 a). This is because films containing larger amounts of CNDs show greater red shifts of the fluorescence emission. The mechanism underlying this phenomenon is the same as that which causes the maximum emission red shift on increasing the concentration of an aqueous solution of CNDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of CND-OBF (0.9 %) was 5.38 %. The letters of the word “Chitosan” and a picture of a real object were both clearly visible through different CND-OBFs under both close and long-distance observation (Fig. 4b), indicating that the introduction of CNDs had an impact on the macroscopic color of CND-OBFs but did not affect its transparency. UV transmittance experiments (Fig. 4c) showed that pure PVA film with transmittance >90 % cannot block light <450 nm. Adding increasing amounts of CNDs to the film markedly enhanced the ability of the film to block <450 nm light; violet light (<400 nm) was completely blocked when the film contained 15.7 % CNDs. In order to study the ability of different CND-OBFs to block UV and blue light, we chose 395 nm (violet light) and 450 nm (blue light) LEDs as the emission sources (Fig. 4d and e). The extent of blocking of 395 nm and 450 nm light gradually increased as the proportion of CNDs in the CND-OBF increased; blocking of violet light and blue light was ∼100 % when the proportion of CNDs in the film was 15.7 % and 21.9 %, respectively. We next investigated the ability of different CND-OBFs to block the harmful blue light emitted by WLEDs and computer and mobile phone screens. As the proportion of CNDs was increased, the blue light peak at 450 nm gradually decreased, whilst the green and red light peaks gradually increased (Figs. S12–S14). Thereby promoting the color change of the light was from blue to orange-red. The CIE1931 color coordinates also showed the transformation from pure white light to orange-red light (Fig. 4f–h). Moreover, under excitation at 365 nm, 405 nm, and 450 nm, the emissions of different CND-OBFs shifted from blue to the region green to red (Fig. S15). At the same time, after passing through CND-OBFs, the violet light peaks at 365 nm and 395 nm LEDs disappeared, and a green-to-red light peak (500–750 nm) appeared (Fig. S16), obtaining an orange-red LED (Fig. S16 inset). The above results all confirmed that the CND-OBFs could absorb short wavelength light (<450 nm) and convert it into green-to-red emission. These results also demonstrated that the CNDs could be used in materials to block short wavelength light [20,23–26]. The extent of blocking of short wavelength light could be adjusted by varying the proportion of CNDs in the film.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.111947

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3

In summary, CNDs with concentration-dependent long wavelength emission properties were synthesized in one step from chitosan. Aqueous solutions of the CNDs showed good stability against photobleaching and had reversible temperature cycle properties. The quantum yield of a solution of CNDs was found to be 6.04 % and the fluorescence lifetime was 7.03 ns. Solutions of the CNDs could be used as a fluorescent ink and for the detection of Fe3+ (LOD = 7.79 μM). CND-OBFs containing different proportions of CNDs were prepared by mixing the CNDs with PVA. The different CND-OBFs all showed good transparency. The fluorescence quantum yield of CND-OBF (0.9 %) was 5.38 %. The ability of the CND-OBFs to block UV (395 nm) and (blue 450 nm) light increased as the proportion of CNDs in the film increased. When the proportions of CNDs were 15.7 % and 21.9 %, respectively, essentially 100 % of violet and blue light was blocked. Further studies showed that CND-OBFs could effectively block the harmful blue light emitted by computer screens, mobile phone screens and WLEDs, confirmed that CND-OBFs had potential applications in blue light filters. As a practical application, CND-OBFs were used to protect wood from damage caused by UV light. As the proportion of CNDs in the CND-OBFs increased, the blocking ability of CND-OBFs to short wavelength light was gradually enhanced. When the proportion of CNDs was more than 15.7 %, the CND-OBFs completely blocked UV light (340 nm, 80W) and prevented the color change of the wood surface caused by short wavelength light. This work provides a new research approach in which CNDs are used to protect wood by blocking short wavelength light.

Bin Zhao: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Visualization. Jiaxin Ni: Methodology, Validation. Yuning Wang: Data curation, Methodology. Lianhang Sun: Data curation, Methodology. Yibing Bai: Data curation, Methodology. Shiyan Han: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhijun Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yahui Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Fugang Gao: Supervision. Wenji Yu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Shujun Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2200601), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171715) and the Hei Long Jiang Postdoctoral Foundation (LBH-Q21003). The authors are grateful for the funding.
